Write a case report of a patient with Vision Loss Due to Methanol Intoxication, which we treated with Hyperbaric Oxygen : A 49 -year old male presented vision loss. The patient was a chronic alcoholic and smoker for last 30 years and there was history of consumption of 4 glass of hommade alcholic liquor. As learned from the patient's medical questionnaire , he had essential hypertension and type 2 diabetes.

On first examination, the patient was conscious but confused. There was diminished light perception in both the eyes. Other cranial nerves were normal. There was no Cerebellar abnormality.

His complete blood count and liver function tests at the time of presentation were normal. Random blood sugar was 282 mg/dl. Test showed blood urea nitrogen of 15,7 mg/dl, creatinine of 0,97 mg/dl, serum sodium 135 mmol/L, and potassium 4,09 mmol/L, AST 66 U/L, ALT 36,6 U/L. Arterial blood gas revealed pH of 7.1, bicarbonate of 9,9 mmol/L, lactate 2,3 mmol/Lpo2 15,3 mm Hg pCo2 29,9 mm Hg. With clinical possibility of methanol toxicity, serum osmolar gap was calculated. It was found to be 23; serum methanol levels were sent which were high with value of 8.04 mmol/L. There were no pathological signs on diffusion weighed Magnetic resonance (MR) imaging and CT.

The patient was admitted to the intensive care unit. During six-day period in intensive care, the patient was hydrated, hemodialysis was done 3 times and given ethanol infusions and sodium bicarbonate . The general condition and biochemical parameters of the patient improved. The patient responded to treatment. Patient's visual acuity improved. Initially at presentation, it was finger-counting (FC+) at 1 m. Now the patient has visual acuity of 6/36 in both eyes without visual aids with minimal focal neurologic deficit and is on regular follow-up.

Give a polished and alluring title include the key text in the title to the abstract                                                     Postmodern and postcolonial feminists have criticized Western feminist scholarship for its tendency to assume that the experiences of Third World women are universally and essentially same, ignoring the diversity and complexity of their lives. In an effort to better understand and give voice to the variety of experiences among Third World women, these feminists have sought to challenge misleading stereotypes that depict them as helpless, submissive, and oppressed. They emphasize that it’s essential to recognize that the lives and experiences of Third World women are shaped by a multitude of factors, including cultural customs, language, history, education, family dynamics, societal values, religion, racial identity, social class, politics and economic status. In this paper, I will explore the ideas of Chandra Talpade Mohanty, a leading postcolonial feminist scholar, and read the novel Women at Point Zero to illustrate how all these factors in combination play a role in shaping the lives and experiences of Third World women. Through this analysis, I aim to challenge Eurocentric narratives and expose their limitations while also showcasing how Firdaus, a character in the novel, challenges and subverts essentialist and stereotypical views of women from the global South as passive and oppressed.

Eurocentrism can be defined as an epistemological framework that situates European culture, history, and values as the defining center of the world, while at the same time disregarding or belittling the contributions of other cultures and civilisations. This ethnocentric positioning perceives the Europe or West as a paragon of universal values and cultural superiority, leading to a systematic undervaluation and misrepresentation of non-European cultures. Consequently, Eurocentric discourse breeds a false dichotomy between the West and non-European societies, perpetuating the notion that the West is superior and in control of all areas of life, including social, political, cultural, scientific, military, and economic fields. Disparaging epithets are often attributed to the “non-West,” reinforcing connotations of inferiority, irrationality, belligerence, otherness, fanaticism, and barbarism in comparison to the West. This paradigm, therefore, not only consolidates the stature of the West, but also perpetuates the subordination of non-European societies within global power structures, and the spheres of knowledge and influence. This belief in Western superiority is often reinforced through various forms of discourse, including history, literature, art, music, etc. In a similar vein, through the utilization of Eurocentric and imperialistic perspectives, academic discourse has consistently depicted ‘Eastern societies’ as exotic and racialized, perpetuating a biased portrayal of the Orient or East.

Please review and optimize the following content:
The development of information science has changed the Zero-
Shot Learning (ZSL) problem, and transferring Internet knowledge
and pre-trained knowledge to learn new categories has become a
common approach. However, this process faces challenges such as noisy labels, class imbalance and data distribution mismatch,
which leads to the performance gaps compared to traditional super-
vised learning methods. We propose a new cross-domain learning
problem - Meta-shot Learning (MSL), and introduce class repre-
sentation and seen class dataset as supervision to address these
issues. We also propose a general-purpose framework with three
modules: Balancer, Featurizer, Tasker. Balancer aligns feature space
and class representation space, and then use it to reduce noise and
evaluate distribution difference. Featurizer generates pseudo fea-
tures for resource-scarce classes guided by Balancer. Finally, Tasker
is trained with original and pseudo features. Our framework out-
performs ZSL and Webly Supervised Learning (WSL) methods on
six benchmark datasets.

Write a polished academic paper in at least four thousand words grounded in literary theoretical framework in more compact way using more powerful words at the beginning of sentences instead of The, this, that, a, consequently etc.
The narrative voice of A Portrait of the Artist as a Young limited yet fluid third-person narrative style, which is closely aligned to the consciousness of Stephen Dedalus. We never get to see inside other characters’ heads; instead, we see them the way Stephen does.. The subjectivity of first-person narration (in the form of a diary at the end) is combined with the objectivity of third-person narration, resulting in a narrative voice that is capable of conveying Stephen’s experiences and thoughts with an immediacy and an intimacy that, until then, had been rarely achieved in the bildungsroman form. Indeed, the book focuses so closely on Stephen, and takes us so deeply into his mind, that at times it resembles a first-person narrative. By doing so, the inner life of Stephen seeks to delineate in such a way that it does not merely serve the unfolding of the story but becomes the story. The narrator presents Stephen’s consciousness and activities in various ways; an important aspect about his narration is that he persistently adapts his style to Stephen’s idiom and mood

However, in some instances, language barriers may hinder equal justice for all, particularly for linguistic minority defendants in the criminal justice system. "How does linguicism impact the experiences of linguistic minority defendants in the criminal justice system?" This research question aims to shed light on the extent and depth of linguicism—the discrimination based on language—and how it intertwines with the legal process and trial outcomes. The hypothesis guiding this research presumes that linguistic minority defendants experience an increased likelihood of negative outcomes within the system, influenced by inadequate language support, limited access to resources, and the unconscious biases of those in positions of authority. The significance of this research is underscored by the potential human rights infringements that can occur when biases based on language impede fair and impartial trials, ultimately undermining the very foundation of the criminal justice system. As the world becomes increasingly interconnected and diverse, addressing this question is crucial to ensuring that society’s quest for justice transcends linguistic barriers."

What was Abbott did here morally right?

 On Friday, a jury unanimously convicted a Texas man of murder in the fatal shooting of an armed protester at a Black Lives Matter protest in 2020. Less than a day later, Texas Gov. Greg Abbott said he will sign a pardon for that man "as soon as it hits my desk." Daniel Perry, a sergeant in the US Army, was working as an Uber driver when he ran a red light and drove into the protest in Austin; some protesters say he nearly ran them down. Garrett Foster, who was carrying an AK-47, approached Perry's car along with other protesters and Perry shot him five times through the window and then drove away, the Austin American-Statesman reports. Perry claims Foster pointed his rifle at him and Perry fired in self-defense, but as the Austin Chronicle reports, that claim was refuted during the trial by multiple witnesses who say Foster did not raise the gun's barrel.

Prosecutors also displayed text messages and social media posts from Perry endorsing violence against protesters. "I might have to kill a few people on my way to work, they are rioting outside my apartment complex," he said in one text. He also pondered the idea of claiming self-defense in such a scenario, which is what he ended up doing after killing Foster. And while the jury did not agree, many on the right came to Perry's defense and issued frantic calls for Abbott to pardon him if he was convicted. "Texas has one of the strongest ‘Stand your ground’ laws of self-defense that cannot be nullified by a jury or progressive district attorney," Abbott said on Twitter, adding that he has requested the Board of Pardons and Paroles to expedite a review of whether Foster should be granted a pardon. He expects a pardon to be recommended, and says he will sign.

Post-Huckleberry Finn, the literary landscape has seen a plethora of works that employ child narrators, each bringing forth unique nuances and thematic concerns. The turn of the twentieth century witnessed a burgeoning interest in the psychological exploration of human experience, catalysed by the ground-breaking theories of Sigmund Freud and other pioneering psychologists of the era. This shift in emphasis from the external to the internal realm of experience, coupled with the Modernist proclivity for experimentation, led to the emergence of innovative narrative techniques that foregrounded the child’s perspective. James Joyce’s “A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man” (1916) exemplify this tendency, as it intricately maps the cognitive development of its protagonist through a process of introspection and self-discovery

Eurocentrism can be defined as an epistemological framework that situates European culture, history, and values as the defining center of the world, while at the same time disregarding or belittling the contributions of other cultures and civilisations. This ethnocentric positioning perceives the Europe or West as a paragon of universal values and cultural superiority, leading to a systematic undervaluation and misrepresentation of non-European cultures. Consequently, Eurocentric discourse breeds a false dichotomy between the West and non-European societies, perpetuating the notion that the West is superior and in control of all areas of life, including social, political, cultural, scientific, military, and economic fields. Disparaging epithets are often attributed to the “non-West,” reinforcing connotations of inferiority, irrationality, belligerence, otherness, fanaticism, and barbarism in comparison to the West. This paradigm, therefore, not only consolidates the stature of the West, but also perpetuates the subordination of non-European societies within global power structures, and the spheres of knowledge and influence. This belief in Western superiority is often reinforced through various forms of discourse, including history, literature, art, music, etc. In a similar vein, through the utilization of Eurocentric and imperialistic perspectives, academic discourse has consistently depicted ‘Eastern societies’ as exotic and racialized, perpetuating a biased portrayal of the Orient or East.

please rewrite this factual paragraph to be about vulnerabilities in machine artificial intelligence: "This phenomenon is called genetic homogeneity or genetic monoculture. It refers to a situation where a population has a limited genetic diversity due to breeding, cloning, or other means of selection. When plants or animals are bred for specific traits or characteristics, such as size, flavor, or resistance to disease, the gene pool can become narrow and uniform. This can make them more susceptible to pests, diseases, and environmental stress. In the case of bananas, the genetic homogeneity of commercial banana crops has made them vulnerable to a fungal disease called Panama disease, which has wiped out entire plantations. Genetic homogeneity can have serious consequences for agriculture and biodiversity, which is why many experts advocate for more diverse breeding programs and conservation efforts."

But it was nothing compared to the gnawing sense of emptiness that consumed him. He tried to push it away, to focus on the task at hand, but it ate away at him like a wolf tearing at its prey. Ever since his father's execution, Robb had been struggling to maintain control over the beast within. Some days were easier than others, but in the end, the wolf always won. He could feel it stirring within him now, even as he tried to act the part of the victorious young king, celebrating his wedding to the beautiful Talisa. But he knew, deep down, that it was all a sham. His victory was hollow, his kingdom divided, and his soul consumed by the darkness that threatened to overtake him at any moment. And as he looked out over the joyous faces of his subjects, he wondered how much longer he could keep up the charade.

Based on Turing’s equation for reaction and diffusion, we can write the following system of partial differential equations:

∂y/∂t = f(y, x) + D∇^2y

∂x/∂t = g(y, x) + C∇^2x

where f(y, x) and g(y, x) are the reaction terms describing the population growth and the information density, respectively, and D and C are the diffusion coefficients for the population and the information density. The Laplacian operator (∇^2) represents the diffusion of the respective variable in space.

For the reaction terms, we can use a logistic growth model for the population:

f(y, x) = ry(1 - y/K)

where r is the intrinsic growth rate, K is the carrying capacity (maximum population density), and y/K is the effect of density-dependent regulation on the population growth.

For the information density term, we can use a linear model:

g(y, x) = ax

where a is a constant that determines the effect of information density on population growth.

检查下面的语法In recent years, the application of machine learning in power analysis attacks has become a hot topic of research. When using machine learning for power analysis attacks, the Hamming weight values output by the cryptographic algorithm SBOX are generally used as labels, and the unbalanced classification of the Hamming weight values output by SBOX leads to an imbalance in the data, which can cause lead to model learning bias. In 2020, DUAN used SMOTE for data augmentation to solve this problem. However, as this approach does not fully consider the distribution characteristics of adjacent samples, it may lead to greater repeatability issues between classes. In this paper, power analysis attack method based on Borderline-SMOTE+XGBoost is proposed. This method synthesizes new samples using minority class samples at the boundary, thereby improving the class distribution of samples and effectively solving the greater repeatability issue between classes in DUAN’s scheme caused by not fully considering the distribution characteristics of adjacent samples. This method can effectively improve the accuracy of the model. And by validating on the DPA V4 dataset, the original data is augmented by this scheme, compared with the scheme of DUAN, there is a maximum 34% improvement in the bit classification accuracy and a corresponding improvement in the model accuracy.

proofread: AI systems like ChatGPT are language models trained with statistical analysis techniques. At their core, their function is to generate human-like text based on patterns learned from large text corpora. Large Language Models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT are based on technologies such as the Transformer architecture and cannot be subjective, conscious, nor capable of human empathy or emotion.

LLMs use neural networks trained on “token” sequences - entities such as words or characters. The model uses probabilities to predict the most plausible continuations of the sequence of tokens. By optimizing these probability estimates, the LLM creates an illusion of coherence and understanding, even though it learns based only on statistical relationships.

In this context, it is important to emphasize that LLMs merely simulate human communication; they do not interpret, create, or understand content in the same way that humans do. Their “knowledge” is the result of identifying patterns in the data input during their training phase.

Because of these fundamental properties of AI systems like ChatGPT, which do not possess subjectivity or consciousness, regulation of AI based on the assumption of potential acquisition of subjectivity and consciousness should be reconsidered. Instead, I recommend that regulatory efforts focus on the actual functioning of these models and the potential impact of their application on society, the economy, and the environment.

To achieve appropriate and effective regulation of AI, the focus should be on specific factors such as transparency, accountability, privacy, fairness, and sustainability.

Sincerely

I've noticed an increase in the amount of buildings and individual households that overwater their lawns during the day, wasting our valuable and depleting water supplies.


While the City of Toronto currently recommends that individuals and businesses water their lawns no more than once per week during off-peak hours (between 11 p.m. and 8 a.m.), this is only a guideline and not an enforceable bylaw (City of Toronto, 2020a). According to your website, the City can publish a 'Outdoor Water Use Advisory' when water supplies become scarce; but, this does not have the same legal heft as a formal bylaw (City of Toronto, 2020b).


According to Survis and Root (2016), lawn watering accounts for 50% to 90% of household water usage in urban areas. Watering lawns during peak daytime hours not only wastes water but also exposes grass to disease-causing evaporation (Survis and Root, 2016). With water demand estimated to climb by 40% by 2050 and water shortages affecting more than half of the world's population by 2025, solving this issue has become critical (Eliasson, 2015).


I strongly urge you to submit an enforceable bylaw prohibiting grass watering between the hours of 8 a.m. and 11 p.m. To help enforce this bylaw, the city should consider recruiting 100 more bylaw officers to actively patrol the city, respond to allegations of infractions, and assure compliance. It is clear that depending on voluntary behavioural change is insufficient, and given the urgency of the situation, we must enact policies that effectively compel residents to practise appropriate water usage.

To make matters worse, new AI-driven start-ups are sprouting every day, compute power is increasing steadily, and the amount of consumer data being collected – and shared – is growing exponentially.

In this backdrop, AI can be used to profile people like you and me to such a detail which may well become uncomfortable! And this is no exaggeration. You may have already had such an experience when having bought a simple T-shirt from a store, you are inundated with personalized recommendations from all sorts of ‘brands’ on the types of clothes you could buy based on your physique or height. Caution: This example is just a tip of the iceberg!

Why regulate AI?

By AI regulations, I refer to the development of laws and policies that broadly govern the algorithms which will help promote responsible use of AI and make businesses accountable.

While AI can be extraordinarily useful to understand consumer preferences and influence customer behavior through targeted messaging, mandatory regulations on AI can go a long way in preventing technology from infringing human rights. They can help ensure that technology is used for the benefit of end users instead of negatively affecting their lives.'

You drag your suitcase behind you, its wheels clacking on the wooden floor. The traditional Japanese residence surrounds you with its calming atmosphere, even in the dreary weather. The soothing scent of fresh tatami mats and the soft light filtering through shoji screens offer a sense of order, although you can’t forget the tension between you and Akura during the journey.

“I still can’t believe you got us lost in that ancient village, Dean,” Akura says, rolling her eyes and dropping her suitcase onto the floor with a thud. She kicks off her shoes and lets out a huff, not as impressed by the surroundings as you are. “Have you never heard of a GPS?”
“It’s not my fault the GPS couldn’t recognize the old streets,” you reply, feeling yourself get defensive before taking a deep breath and grounding yourself. You try to focus on the peacefulness of the room, hoping it’ll calm Akura down too.

“Well, we’re here now, aren’t we?” you say, attempting diplomacy as you start to unpack.
Akura glares at the two separate futons on the floor, placed far apart from each other. “It doesn’t change the fact that it took us far longer than necessary to get here.” She pokes your chest playfully.

Not wanting to argue, you swallow your retort and instead focus on settling in. The interior of the residence is stunning, with simple decorations that add a touch of elegance - scrolls on the walls, a low table with short-legged chairs, and a subtle scent of green tea that fills the air.

Later, after dinner, you both head back to the residence. The rain outside has intensified, and any lingering tension between you and Akura seems to have come indoors.

“You know,” she says, a sly smile on her face. “You’re not a bad date. Which one of the department girls are you going out with?“

“None. Never had one.”

Using Turing’s reaction-diffusion model to describe human population growth, we can write a set of coupled partial differential equations for the evolution of population density P and information density I:

∂P/∂t = Dp ∇^2 P + f(P, I)
∂I/∂t = Di ∇^2 I + g(P, I)

where Dp and Di are the diffusion coefficients for population and information, ∇^2 is the Laplacian operator, and f and g are the reaction terms that describe the interactions between the two populations.

We assume that population density promotes the diffusion of information, and that information density promotes population growth. This can be captured by the following non-linear functions:

f(P, I) = rP(1 - P/K)I
g(P, I) = aI

where r is the intrinsic growth rate of the population, K is the carrying capacity. linearize the system, find the eigenvalues and the steady-states solutions of the system. explain the perturbations of the system

The three parameters of a PID controller, namely the proportional gain (KP), integral gain (KI), and derivative gain (KD), have distinct effects on the closed-loop system. The proportional gain determines the system's immediate response to a change in error. Increasing KP accelerates the system's response and reduces the steady-state error, but a higher KP can also cause overshoot and instability in the system. The integral gain corrects any steady-state error in the system. An increased KI decreases the steady-state error, but it can also cause instability and overshoot in the system. The derivative gain reduces overshoot and dampens oscillations caused by the proportional gain. A higher KD improves the stability of the system and reduces overshoot, but it can also cause the system to respond more slowly. Therefore, selecting appropriate values for these gains depends on the specific requirements of the system and requires careful consideration of the desired tradeoff between performance and stability.

"Using all digital tools at my disposal, I sought to find multiple research papers on the subject, organize them cleanly, and use them appropriately. I’ll go into detail as to how they’ll come to be helpful towards finding an answer on a different section of this document.

The main drawback, or, better put, limitation in finding these was that I had to restrain myself to only four documents from which to draw from. This added the extra bit of challenge of having to prioritize some documents above others.

Because of this, I sought to put emphasis on two main defining aspects when it came to choosing each document; Time of publishing, and relevance. Relevance was the hardest one to garner, but I managed to do so by first looking into each author’s background, until I eventually came to the informed conclusion that I didn’t need to verify the credibility of each author.

Thus I settled on them having a sizeable number of citations in other papers and being from different time periods that were relatively recent for the sake of thoroughness.

The first draft of this paper was written on a note-taking program called Obsidian.md, then machine translated from its original English, (mainly because it’s the language I feel the most proficient and comfortable writing in and this workflow has become customary for me after applying it for over less than half a decade,) edited the output for the sake of clarity and quality control, and then moved the text content onto Google Docs for formatting."

Eurocentrism can be defined as an epistemological framework that situates European culture, history, and values as the defining center of the world, while at the same time disregarding or belittling the contributions of other cultures and civilisations. This ethnocentric positioning perceives the Europe or West as a paragon of universal values and cultural superiority, leading to a systematic undervaluation and misrepresentation of non-European cultures. Consequently, Eurocentric discourse breeds a false dichotomy between the West and non-European societies, perpetuating the notion that the West is superior and in control of all areas of life, including social, political, cultural, scientific, military, and economic fields. Disparaging epithets are often attributed to the “non-West,” reinforcing connotations of inferiority, irrationality, belligerence, otherness, fanaticism, and barbarism in comparison to the West. This paradigm, therefore, not only consolidates the stature of the West, but also perpetuates the subordination of non-European societies within global power structures, and the spheres of knowledge and influence. This belief in Western superiority is often reinforced through various forms of discourse, including history, literature, art, music, etc. In a similar vein, through the utilization of Eurocentric and imperialistic perspectives, academic discourse has consistently depicted ‘Eastern societies’ as exotic and racialized, perpetuating a biased portrayal of the Orient or East.

paraphrase the text: Space and time are the basic forms of the existence of matter. In these forms are all existing objects that cannot exist except in space and time.
Many sciences deal with the problem of space and time. As philosophical categories, space and time are considered, firstly, as objective properties of the real world, displayed by our consciousness, and, secondly, as attributes of matter.
Space is "a form of existence of matter, characterized by such properties as extension, structure, coexistence and interaction. Space is, first of all, the mutual arrangement of things and processes next to each other, their extension and a certain order of interconnection. It is three-dimensional and reversible."
Time is "a form of the existence of matter, expressing the duration of existence and the sequence of changes in the states of all material systems and processes in the world. The main properties of time include duration, change, development. Time is one-dimensional and irreversible."
The problem of the relationship between the categories of matter, space and time is considered in two main concepts - substantial (from Latin substantia - what underlies; essence; Democritus, Epicurus, Newton) and relational (from Latin relatio - relation; Aristotle, Leibniz, Einstein).

Добавить код к макросу, чтобы тот проверял данные с копируемых листов и удалял повторы строк. Sub CopySheets() Dim SourceWorkbook As Workbook Dim SourceSheet As Worksheet Dim TargetWorkbook As Workbook Dim TargetSheet As Worksheet Dim SourceAddress As String Dim lastRow As Long Dim LastTargetRow As Long Dim SheetName As String ' Установите книгу, из которой копируются данные, и книгу, в которую вставляются данные Set TargetWorkbook = ThisWorkbook Set TargetSheet = TargetWorkbook.Worksheets("Лист6") ' Получите адрес исходной книги из Лист5, ячейка A1 SourceAddress = TargetWorkbook.Worksheets("Лист5").Range("A1").Value Set SourceWorkbook = Workbooks.Open(SourceAddress) ' Обход всех листов исходной книги For Each SourceSheet In SourceWorkbook.Worksheets ' Проверьте, содержит ли имя листа слово "Ашан" If InStr(1, SourceSheet.Name, "Ашан", vbTextCompare) > 0 Then ' Найдите последнюю строку в исходном листе и целевом листе lastRow = SourceSheet.Cells(SourceSheet.Rows.Count, "A").End(xlUp).Row LastTargetRow = TargetSheet.Cells(TargetSheet.Rows.Count, "A").End(xlUp).Row ' Копируйте данные из исходного листа в целевой лист SourceSheet.Range("A1:Z" & lastRow).Copy TargetSheet.Range("B" & LastTargetRow + 1) ' Запишите имя скопированного листа в столбец A целевого листа TargetSheet.Range("A" & LastTargetRow + 1 & ":A" & LastTargetRow + lastRow).Value = SourceSheet.Name End If Next SourceSheet ' Закройте исходную книгу без сохранения изменений SourceWorkbook.Close SaveChanges:=False End Sub

reicht der folgende text jemanden zu überzeigen der denkt dass KIs nd LLMs bewusstsein erlangen können? : AI systems like ChatGPT are language models that utilize statistical analysis techniques for training. These systems generate human-like text by learning patterns from large text corpora. ChatGPT is an example of Large Language Models (LLMs) that use the Transformer architecture and are incapable of human empathy or emotion.

LLMs depend on neural networks trained on “token” sequences like words or characters. These networks predict the most probable continuations of the sequence of tokens using probabilities. This optimization of probability estimates creates the illusion of coherence and comprehension, even though LLMs rely solely on statistical relationships for learning.

It is necessary to stress that LLMs only simulate human conversation and do not interpret, create, or understand content in the same way as humans. Their “knowledge” results from recognizing patterns in the data input during their training phase.

Given these essential characteristics of AI systems such as ChatGPT, which lack subjectivity or consciousness, there needs to be a reconsideration of regulating AI based on the possibility of acquiring subjectivity and consciousness. Instead, regulatory measures are required for particular factors such as transparency, accountability, privacy, fairness, and sustainability.

Best Regards.

Without the talent, these increased investments in artificial intelligence and machine learning could be wasted, leading to financial losses and unrealized opportunities. Three-quarters of respondents want to train and upskill existing staff, compared to 64% who want to recruit new talent. Training and upskilling may prove more cost-effective compared to hiring and using contractors – but the study highlighted barriers such as lack of time and motivation, and belief that senior management may be worried about people taking their skills elsewhere.

Give a polished alluring title, include the key text in the title                                                                                                            Postmodern and postcolonial feminists have criticized Western feminist scholarship for its tendency to assume that the experiences of Third World women are universally and essentially same, ignoring the diversity and complexity of their lives. In an effort to better understand and give voice to the variety of experiences among Third World women, these feminists have sought to challenge misleading stereotypes that depict them as helpless, submissive, and oppressed. They emphasize that it’s essential to recognize that the lives and experiences of Third World women are shaped by a multitude of factors, including cultural customs, language, history, education, family dynamics, societal values, religion, racial identity, social class, politics and economic status. In this paper, I will explore the ideas of Chandra Talpade Mohanty, a leading postcolonial feminist scholar, and read the novel Women at Point Zero to illustrate how all these factors in combination play a role in shaping the lives and experiences of Third World women. Through this analysis, I aim to challenge Eurocentric narratives and expose their limitations while also showcasing how Firdaus, a character in the novel, challenges and subverts essentialist and stereotypical views of women from the global South as passive and oppressed.

Ich schreibe eine Bachelorarbeit auf dem höchsten akademischen Niveau. Das Thema ist das folgende: Real Estate Investment Trusts and Institutional Ownership
Description:
Investing in real estate has always required a capital cushion and access to financing. The U.S. Congress created Real Estate Investment Trusts (in short REITs) in 1960 in order to give individual investors the possibility to participate in large-scale and diversified income-producing real estate investments. The REIT approach has not only proven its worth but has also established itself as a popular investment vehicle over the decades. According to research by the National Association of Real Estate Investment Trusts (Nareit), almost every second American household owns REIT stocks. REITs are also enjoying popularity outside the United States. To date, 40 countries have introduced REIT regimes. Although created for “small” investor, institutional investors are increasingly enjoying investments in REITs. The involvement of institutional investors brings many advantages but also risks. The aim of this study is to assess the impact of institutional involvement on REITs. Moreover, the study should investigate whether and why changes in institutional ownership have a positive or negative impact on firm returns.
Reference literature:
Chung, R., S. Fung, and S.Y.K. Hung (2012). Institutional Investors and Firm Efficiency of Real Estate Investment Trusts. The Journal of Real Estate Finance and Economics 45, 171-211.
Devos, E., S.E. Ong, A.C. Spieler, and D. Tsang (2013). REIT institutional ownership dynamics and the financial crisis. The Journal of Real Estate Finance and Economics, 47(2), 266-288.

Develop this idea for a casual restaurant simulation game: The player controls a small diner which serves breakfast and lunch. The objective is to seat customers, take orders, deliver food and provide good service while managing the restaurant's resources effectively. Some key elements of the game: • Tables: There will be a finite number of tables that can seat 2-4 customers each. Tables can be occupied or vacant. • Reservation system: Customers can reserve tables in advance. The player has to manage the reservation book and assign reserved tables to reserved customers. Walk-ins also arrive randomly. • Menu: There will be options for breakfast (pancakes, omelets, sandwiches), lunch (burgers, salads, soups) and drinks (coffee, juice, soda). Random "specials" can also be generated daily. • Customers: Customers will call in for reservations, walk in, make ordering requests, and provide feedback on their experience. Their priority can range from regulars to first-timers. • Staff: The player acts as the manager, seating customers and assigning waiting staff to tables to take orders and serve food. More staff can be hired as the restaurant grows. • Resources: There will be limited kitchen resources, staff, food inventory and an operational budget. Reframe and rephrase in polished language using  literary jargon and Avoid repeating words or ideas 
The narrative voice of A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man is remarkable for its limited yet fluid third-person narrative style, which is closely aligned to the consciousness of Stephen Dedalus. We never get to see inside other characters’ heads; instead, we see them the way Stephen does.. The subjectivity of first-person narration (in the form of a diary at the end) is combined with the objectivity of third-person narration, resulting in a narrative voice that is capable of conveying Stephen’s experiences and thoughts with an immediacy and an intimacy that, until then, had been rarely achieved in the bildungsroman form. Indeed, the book focuses so closely on Stephen, and takes us so deeply into his mind, that at times it resembles a first-person narrative. By doing so, the inner life of Stephen seeks to delineate in such a way that it does not merely serve the unfolding of the story but becomes the story. The narrator presents Stephen’s consciousness and activities in various ways; an important aspect about his narration is that he persistently adapts his style to Stephen’s idiom and mood

Post-Huckleberry Finn, the literary landscape has seen a plethora of works that employ child narrators, each bringing forth unique nuances and thematic concerns. The turn of the twentieth century witnessed a burgeoning interest in the psychological exploration of human experience, catalyzed by the groundbreaking theories of Sigmund Freud and other pioneering psychologists of the era. This shift in emphasis from the external to the internal realm of experience, coupled with the Modernist proclivity for experimentation, led to the emergence of innovative narrative techniques that foregrounded the child’s perspective. James Joyce’s “A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man” (1916) exemplify this tendency, as they intricately map the cognitive development of their respective protagonists through a process of introspection and self-discovery.

The term “third world” was originally used during the Cold War era to refer to countries aligned with neither the capitalist or NATO First World nor with communist or Soviet Union Second World blocs. Over time, the term has, however, taken on a broader meaning and is now commonly used to describe developing countries in Africa, Asia, and Latin America. These countries are often characterised by economic and social underdevelopment, political instability, limited access to education and healthcare, and high poverty rates. Despite its historical use, the term is now considered politically incorrect and potentially offensive as it implies a hierarchy of development and reinforces negative stereotypes about these regions of the world. This negative stereotyping of Third-World countries is also evident in the way Western feminists have portrayed Third-World women. Therefore, for this paper, the term “Third World women” will be used in the manner Western feminists employ it in their written works.

The presence of missing values in the data objects is ubiquitous in practice. Missing entries could be due to various types of faults during data collection, data transfer, or data cleaning phase and may occur arbitrarily. Dealing with a data set with missing values is one of the infamous challenges in data science. The problems of filling up missing entries are often cast as optimization tasks. One such problem is the following:

You are given an m×n matrix M with each entry set to either 0 or 1 or a wildcard ∗. Furthermore, all the entries of the last (i.e., mth) row of M are set to wildcards (i.e., ∗). You are also given a non-negative integer r. The problem is to decide whether there exists an m × n matrix M¯ ∈ {0, 1} m×n (i.e., with no ∗-entry) such that

For all (i, j) ∈ {1, 2, · · · , m} × {1, 2, · · · , n} with M[i][j] ̸= ∗, M¯ [i][j] = M[i][j], and

The number of entries that are different between the last row and any other row in M¯ is at most r. More formally, for every i ∈ {1, 2, · · · , m − 1},
{j ∈ {1, 2, · · · , n} | M¯ [i][j] ̸= M¯ [m][j]} ≤ r

a) [5 marks] Show that the above problem is in NP hard.
Take any 3-SAT instance with n variables and m clauses. From that, construct an (m + 1) * n matrix by creating a column for each variable and a row for each clause. The last row is the "answer" row for the 3-SAT problem while m rows are just the number of clauses

解释这一段：Could grid-cell firing be detected in the functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) signal, which reflects changes in metabolic activity across thousands of individual neurons9? The grid patterns of neighbouring cells are offset so as to ‘tile’ the environment1, making systematic variation in population activity with position unlikely. However, three factors may allow detection using fMRI. The first factor is that the angular orientation of the grids relative to the environment appears to be constant across cells, whether they are neighbouring1 or farther apart5 (Supplementary Fig. 1), and rotate coherently when salient distal cues are rotated1,6. The other two factors result from new analyses presented here. The second factor being that firing of ‘conjunctive’ grid cells, found in the deeper layers of entorhinal cortex6 and in pre- and parasubiculum7, is modulated by running direction6. Here we show that the directions of modulation of conjunctive grid cells are aligned with the main axes of the grids (Fig. 1a–c). These first two factors will create systematic differences in neural population dynamics for runs aligned or misaligned with the main axes of the grids (Fig. 2). A third factor is running speed: the rate6 and inter-burst frequency10 of grid-cell firing, and the frequency with which individual firing fields in a grid are sampled, increase with running speed. In addition, we show that the spatial organization of grid-cell firing is more strongly apparent during fast running than during slow running and immobility (Fig. 1d; see Methods for details and Supplementary Figs 2–6 for further analyses and recording locations). Thus, entorhinal grid cells form a coherent population in which the common effects of orientation and speed of movement could produce a macroscopic signal visible with fMRI.

the intuitive considerations reported above, we can now move to a precise small-signal Laplace-
domain analysis, that might be very useful for a clear understanding of control limitations and
delay effects implied by the uniformly sampled PWM.
An equivalent model of the uniformly sampled PWM process is represented in Fig. 2.6(a).
As can be seen, the schematic diagram adopts the typical continuous time model of a sampled
data system, where an ideal sampler is followed by a zero-order-hold (ZOH). e quantization
effect that is associated, in the physical implementation of the modulator of Fig. 2.5, to the dig-
ital counter and binary comparator operation, is neglected, being irrelevant from the dynamic
response delay standpoint. Accordingly, in the model of Fig. 2.6(a), after the modulating signal
m.t/ is processed by the ZOH, the PWM waveform is generated by an ideal analog comparator,
that compares the ZOH output signal ms .t/ and the carrier waveform c.t /.
Depending on c.t/, several different uniformly-sampled pulse-width modulators can be
obtained. For example, in Fig. 2.6(b) a trailing-edge modulation is depicted, where the update of
the modulating signal is performed at the beginning of the modulation period. Note that this is

翻译为中文：User Consent for Data Collection, Use, and Sharing
▼
By using our app, which is powered by OpenAI's API, you acknowledge and agree to the following terms regarding the data you provide:

Collection: We may collect information, including the inputs you type into our app, the outputs generated by OpenAI's API, and certain technical details about your device and connection (such as browser type, operating system, and IP address) provided by your device's request headers.
Use: We may use the collected data for research purposes, to improve our services, and to develop new products or services, including commercial applications, and for security purposes, such as protecting against unauthorized access and attacks.
Sharing and Publication: Your data, including the technical details collected from your device's request headers, may be published, shared with third parties, or used for analysis and reporting purposes.
Data Retention: We may retain your data, including the technical details collected from your device's request headers, for as long as necessary.
By continuing to use our app, you provide your explicit consent to the collection, use, and potential sharing of your data as described above. If you do not agree with our data collection, use, and sharing practices, please do not use our app.

Rust’s PASETO library provides the validate_local_token function which is used to validate a local PASETO JSON Web Token (JWT) token.

To explain this function, let’s break it down:

1. The function takes three arguments: the token value, a secret key used to sign and verify the token, and an optional token purpose value (default is “local”).

2. The function first decodes the token into its header, payload, and signature parts using the decode function.

3. Next, it verifies that the token version is “v2.local” and that the token purpose matches the provided value (or “local” by default).

4. It then computes the signature of the token using the provided secret key and the token header and payload data.

5. Finally, it compares this computed signature with the signature in the original token to ensure that they match. If they do not match, the function returns an error indicating that the token is invalid.

In summary, validate_local_token checks that the locally signed PASETO JWT has a valid version, purpose, and signature using a provided secret key.

quote directly from some academic sources with citations which backup the following claims: The Greek debt crisis is a recent example of the cycle of debt accumulation and default. Greece joined the Eurozone in 2001, and the government embarked on a program of borrowing to finance public spending. The government borrowed heavily to finance infrastructure projects, social programs, and public employment. The private sector also borrowed heavily to finance consumption, real estate, and financial speculation. This led to a cycle of debt accumulation, which was sustained by low-interest rates and high-risk tolerance.

However, the profitability of these activities declined in the wake of the global financial crisis of 2008. This led to a decline in investment, production, employment, income, and wealth, and a rise in unemployment, poverty, and inequality. The government was unable to repay its debts, and the private sector defaulted on its loans. This led to a crisis of confidence in the Greek economy, which led to a rise in interest rates, a fall in investment, a decline in production, and an increase in poverty, unemployment, and social unrest.

The Greek debt crisis highlights the contradictions of capitalism. The cycle of debt accumulation and default is a result of the contradictions between the profit motive and the need for social welfare, between the global economy and the national economy, and between the financial sector and the real economy. The Greek government tried to address this crisis by imposing austerity measures, which led to a decline in public spending, social welfare, and employment. However, this only exacerbated the crisis by reducing the purchasing power of consumers and the profitability of capitalists.

Write a case report of  this  patient with Vision Loss Due to Methanol Intoxication, which we treated with Hyperbaric Oxygen : A 49 -year old male presented vision loss. The patient was a chronic alcoholic and smoker for last 30 years and there was history of consumption of 4 glass of hommade alcholic liquor. As learned from the patient’s medical questionnaire , he had essential hypertension and type 2 diabetes.

On first examination, the patient was conscious but confused. There was diminished light perception in both the eyes. Other cranial nerves were normal. There was no Cerebellar abnormality.

His complete blood count and liver function tests at the time of presentation were normal. Random blood sugar was 282 mg/dl. Test showed blood urea nitrogen of 15,7 mg/dl, creatinine of 0,97 mg/dl, serum sodium 135 mmol/L, and potassium 4,09 mmol/L, AST 66 U/L, ALT 36,6 U/L. Arterial blood gas revealed pH of 7.1, bicarbonate of 9,9 mmol/L, lactate 2,3 mmol/Lpo2 15,3 mm Hg pCo2 29,9 mm Hg. With clinical possibility of methanol toxicity, serum osmolar gap was calculated. It was found to be 23; serum methanol levels were sent which were high with value of 8.04 mmol/L. There were no pathological signs on diffusion weighed Magnetic resonance (MR) imaging and CT.

The patient was admitted to the intensive care unit. During six-day period in intensive care, the patient was hydrated, hemodialysis was done 3 times and given ethanol infusions and sodium bicarbonate . The general condition and biochemical parameters of the patient improved. The patient responded to treatment. Patient’s visual acuity improved. Initially at presentation, it was finger-counting (FC+) at 1 m. Now the patient has visual acuity of 6/36 in both eyes without visual aids with minimal focal neurologic deficit and is on regular follow-up.

Hey GPT, I'm learning about the Hugging Face transformers library, and was wondering.. what are those "heads" they are talking about? For example, you pass a text to the tokenizer, or can pass multiple at once as an array/dictionary - but with the "QuestionAnswering" one, the tokenizer takes two text inputs for one query (in the tutorial dubbed "question" and "text", but it is the same tokenizer. How does this work? Is the "head" some kind of mechanism in this case, directing two inputs to the same model in sequence to get the expected result? Thanks.

From Hugging Face:

OPTForSequenceClassification:
"The OPT Model transformer with a sequence classification head on top (linear layer)."

OPTForQuestionAnswering:
"The OPT Model transformer with a span classification head on top for extractive question-answering tasks like SQuAD (a linear layers on top of the hidden-states output to compute span start logits and span end logits)."

Diffusion of reactants to the active surface
Adsorption of one or more reactants onto the surface
Surface reaction
Desorption of products from the surface
Diffusion of products away from the surface

summarise this: Three conclusions flow from this onomastic survey. First, names based on Hermanubis stand somewhat apart from other polytheophoric names common in the same period in Roman Egypt because, unlike the latter, they refer to a specific and independently attested god: a hybrid-named Hellenized version of Anubis. This is visible in the "onomastic behaviour" of Hermanubis names, such as their receptivity to various suffixes and their composition with names of other gods in "proper" polytheophoric formations. Second, the precise chronological and geographical overlap between the onomastic evidence and the representations of the fully anthropomorphic Alexandrian god known conventionally as "Hermanubis" supports art historians' designation of the latter as "Hermanubis" (for which there is otherwise no positive support; see above). Finally, the connection of Hermanubis with Cynopolis evidenced by some of the personal names, and especially the connection with a priest of Anubis, suggests that Hermanubis continued to be closely associated with the traditional Anubis. It may not be warranted, therefore, to separate too forcefully, in the manner of Jean-Claude Grenier (1990: 265, 268), the more traditional funerary Anubis of the Isiac cult from a more "universalizing" Hermanubis.[22]

x(t) represents the density of information in the environment, which can affect the rate of population growth.
y(t) represents the density of the population at time t.
Based on Turing’s equation for reaction and diffusion, we can write the following system of partial differential equations:

∂y/∂t = f(y, x) + D∇^2y

∂x/∂t = g(y, x) + C∇^2x

where f(y, x) and g(y, x) are the reaction terms describing the population growth and the information density, respectively, and D and C are the diffusion coefficients for the population and the information density. The Laplacian operator (∇^2) represents the diffusion of the respective variable in space.

For the reaction terms, we can use a logistic growth model for the population:

f(y, x) = ryx(1 - y/K)

where r is the intrinsic growth rate, K is the carrying capacity (maximum population density), and y/K is the effect of density-dependent regulation on the population growth.

For the information density term, we can use a linear model:

g(y, x) = ay-bx

where a and b are constants that determine the effect of information density on population growth linearize the system, find the eigenvalues and determine the stability of the system

The term “third world” was originally used during the Cold War era to refer to countries aligned with neither the capitalist or NATO First World nor with communist or Soviet Union Second World blocs. Over time, the term has, however, taken on a broader meaning and is now commonly used to describe developing countries in Africa, Asia, and Latin America. These countries are often characterised by economic and social underdevelopment, political instability, limited access to education and healthcare, and high poverty rates. Despite its historical use, the term is now considered politically incorrect and potentially offensive as it implies a hierarchy of development and reinforces negative stereotypes about these regions of the world. This negative stereotyping of Third-World countries is also evident in the way Western feminists have portrayed Third-World women. Therefore, for this paper, the term “Third World women” will be used in the manner Western feminists employ it in their written works.

summarise this: 	There are three forms of the personal name based on Hermanubis in papyri and inscriptions from Egypt, in addition to two "proper" polytheophoric names. (In four instances [1, 5, 17, 21] the ending of the name is abbreviated or not preserved.) The form Ἑρμάνουβιϲ, attested six times (2, 9, 11, 13, 14, 15), simply replicates the god's name without further ado, through the phenomenon of "direct theonymy" (the giving to humans of unaltered divine names) that became widespread in the Roman period.[8] In an odd instance (9) the form Ἑρμανουβιϲ in the nominative is borne by a woman, an Alexandrian nurse (ἡ τροφόϲ).[9] It is highly unusual for female theophoric names to be unmarked for gender in this way.[10] I suspect that because the element -ανουβιϲ in Ἑρμάνουβιϲ was often declined with a dental declension in -ιδ-, it might have been conflated with the similar declension of Greek feminine names in -ίϲ, -ίδοϲ.[11] If this is correct, it may be preferable to read the nurse's name as an oxytone (Ἑρμανουβίϲ). There are two further forms based on the god Hermanubis: Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ (six instances: 3, 4, 7, 8, 12, 20) and Ἑρμανουβίων (four instances: 3, 6, 18, 19). -ᾶϲ and -ίων were, in fact, the commonest Greek suffixes for personal names based on the Egyptian god Ἄνουβιϲ.[12] It is interesting that in a business letter (3) the two forms Ἑρμανουβίων (l.38) and Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ (l.41) are used interchangeably to designate the same individual. In this case, Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ is probably a hypocoristic of Ἑρμανουβίων, since the suffix -ᾶϲ often served this purpose.[13]

this the last conversation: do you creon from greek mythology
Creon is a figure from Greek mythology who appears in several stories and plays, including Sophocles’ “Antigone” and “Oedipus Rex.” He is primarily known as the king of Thebes and Oedipus’ brother-in-law.

Creon is the brother of Jocasta, Oedipus’ wife and mother. In “Oedipus Rex,” he begins as a trusted advisor to Oedipus, but as the tragic events unfold and it becomes clear that Oedipus has unknowingly killed his father and married his mother, Creon comes into a position of power. After the revelation of the terrible truth about Oedipus’ birth, Jocasta takes her own life, and Oedipus blinds himself and goes into exile. Creon then becomes the ruler of Thebes.

In “Antigone,” Creon serves as the play’s primary antagonist. The story takes place after the deaths of Oedipus’ sons, Eteocles, and Polynices, who have killed each other in a battle for the throne. Creon decrees that Eteocles, who died defending Thebes, should receive a proper burial, while Polynices, who attacked the city, should be left unburied which is a great dishonor in ancient Greek culture.

Antigone, Oedipus’ daughter, and Polynices’ sister defy Creon’s order and secretly buries her brother. When Creon discovers her defiance, he orders Antigone to be executed. However, his decision leads to a series of tragic events in which his own son, Haemon, who is in love with Antigone, takes his own life, followed soon after by Creon’s wife, Eurydice, who commits suicide as well.

Throughout these plays, Creon is portrayed as a proud and stubborn ruler who clings to his authority and makes decisions without considering the consequences. His tragic downfall ultimately results from his unwillingness to listen and learn from others, particularly the wise advice of the prophet Tiresias who attempts to warn him against his actions.

翻译：In this unexplored temperature regime, the photonic link exhibits noiselevels approaching microwave vacuum fluctuations, enabling the control and measurement of highly coherent quantum states. In our approach, the microwave control signals are upconverted to the optical frequency domain using electro-optic modulators (EOMS), guided along optical fibres to the cryogenic environment of the processor and downconverted back to microwave frequencies using high-speed photodetection®, see Fig.1b. The vanishing thermalconductivity of optical fibres at low temperatureand their largeintrinsicbandwidth would enable the delivery of millions of control signals directly to the millikelvin stage with no substantial passive heat load. To evaluate this photonic link, we operate a commercial high-speed photodiode at 20 mK to control and measure a superconducting transmon qubit. We demonstrate theability to perform high-fidelity single-shot qubit readout and fast qubit gates while maintaining quantum coherence. We then exploit the extreme noisesensitivity ofthe qubit to measure the photodiode noise at sub-microampere photocurrents, revealing shot-noise-limited performance. Finally, we consider the noise and heat dissipation of the photodiode together to predict the scalability of our approach, charting apathtowards scaling wellbeyond the capability oftraditional coaxial wiring

Be sure to follow the format of theory application: State a key point of the theory and then provide an example from your own life that proves it to be true. Explain how the theory and example are linked.

Application of Psychosocial Theory to my own life:

According to the psychosocial theory, individuals pass through the stage of identity vs. role confusion, where they try to figure out their personal identity and how to fit into society. A key point of this theory is that individuals may experience identity conflict and change in their lives.

In my own life, I faced a situation where I had to move from the country in which I was born to Canada for my education. This transition made me experience identity conflict due to the difference in cultural values, making me question my beliefs and priorities. I felt unsure about my role; however, with time, I adjusted and settled into the new environment, forming a new balance between my cultural identity and fitting into the new place. This example proves the psychosocial theory to be true as I went through a phase of identity conflict and eventually resolved it.

The theory and example are linked because the psychosocial theory explains the process of identity conflict resolution. In my case, the conflict was resolved by understanding the differences and finding a balance between my cultural identity and adapting to the new environment. This allowed me to grow and learn new perspectives in life.
